---
title: "A grammar of sea-going observational data"
number-sections: true
toc: true
toc-location: right
toc-depth: 3
bibliography: references.bib
---

@yang2025 propose a grammar of data analysis in which any analysis can be expressed as computing
*metrics* over *dimensions* — a layer of abstraction above the grammar of data manipulation
(dplyr, pandas) that brings the vocabulary of the code into alignment with the intent of the
analyst. Their framework presupposes, however, that the underlying data is already consistently
structured: that the "dimensions" the analyst has in mind correspond to coherently named and keyed
columns in the tables being queried. This document proposes the complementary layer below: a
grammar of *data representation* for vessel-based sea-going observations. Before any metric can be
computed over any dimension, the raw records from dozens of national and institutional schemas must
be mapped onto a common hierarchy — voyages, discrete operations, specific observational acts,
individual measurements — connected by stable keys and a controlled column vocabulary. Getting
that representation right is the precondition for the clean metric-over-dimension analysis that
@yang2025 envision.

## Motivation

In 2010, Hadley Wickham formalised the **grammar of graphics** in R through
ggplot2 [@wickham2010]. The central insight was not aesthetic but structural:
any statistical graphic, regardless of type or complexity, can be decomposed
into a small set of composable primitives — data, aesthetic mappings, geometric
objects, scales, facets, and a coordinate system. Give those primitives
consistent names and rules, and you can describe every plot in the same
language.

Sea-going observational data has an analogous problem. Every country, every
regulatory body, every research institute that sends vessels to sea has its own
schema. Column names differ (`visir`, `station_id`, `VE_REF`, `LE_CDAT`,
`stnno`, `haul_id`). Coordinate encodings differ: DDMMss integers, DDM floats,
decimal degrees, ICES rectangles (~50 km × ~50 km). Gear and instrument
vocabularies differ. Time representations differ. Yet the underlying reality
being recorded is always the same: *a vessel leaves port, makes a series of
observations at sea — deploying gear, lowering instruments, or simply steaming
on transect — and returns to port.*

But naming inconsistency is only half the problem. The deeper issue is one of
**resolution**: some systems record individual operations with precise GPS
positions and event-level timestamps; others aggregate many such operations
into a single daily entry attributed to an area the size of a county. The same
four-level hierarchy underlies every system — the difference is how finely each
level is resolved.

The grammar tradition has since broadened: Wickham's dplyr formalised a
grammar of *data manipulation* [@wickham2010], and @yang2025 propose a
grammar of *data analysis* built on top of it. Each layer assumes the one
below is sound. This document addresses the foundational layer — a grammar
of *data representation* — for vessel-based sea-going observations.

This document proposes a minimal, unambiguous table structure — a grammar —
that can represent any vessel-based sea-going observation, regardless of
whether the vessel is a commercial fishing boat, a fisheries research vessel,
or an oceanographic survey ship, and regardless of its country of origin or
degree of spatial and temporal resolution. Commercial fisheries logbooks
(Icelandic and EU/ICES formats) and scientific survey data serve as the initial
test cases. The grammar is intentionally broader: it is designed to accommodate
hydrographic casts, acoustic transects, biological sampling, and continuous
underway recording alongside fishing operations, all within the same structural
framework.

Commercial fishing logbooks are currently the most thoroughly documented sample
type in this document, reflecting the project's immediate focus. Documentation
for other observation types will be added as the work develops.

## The hierarchy

Vessel-based sea-going activity is naturally hierarchical. Four levels cover
essentially all cases:

```
Trip
 └── Station  (one or more per trip)
      └── Sample  (one or more per station)
           └── Observation / Measurement  (one or more per sample)
```

The relationship is strictly one-to-many at each level: one trip may contain
many stations; one station may contain many samples; one sample may contain
many individual observations or measurements. Foreign keys flow downward only;
each child record carries the identifier of its parent.

The same structure accommodates commercial fishing vessels and scientific
research vessels equally. Both consist of a vessel making a voyage during which
operations are carried out at discrete locations. The grammar accommodates both;
the difference lies in the *type and richness* of the sample and measurement
tables — not in the structure of the trip and station tables.



### What counts as a station?

A station is any location or interval during a trip where the vessel engages in
activity other than normal transit — gear deployment, scientific sampling, or
any other deliberate operation. Transit legs between stations are not stations.
This definition is deliberately broad: a CTD cast, a plankton net tow, or a
hydroacoustic transect all qualify as stations just as a trawl haul does.

Footnote: Historically exact coordinates were not reported, hence in the station
table one includes any kind of variable that gives approximate location.

### Multiple sample types at one station

A single station can yield several distinct samples collected simultaneously or
in close succession. In scientific survey work this is the norm rather than the
exception:

```
Station (trawl haul at 64°N, 25°W)
 ├── Fishing sample      → catch measurements (species, weight, length)
 ├── Hydrographic sample → CTD cast (depth, temperature, salinity profile)
 └── Plankton sample     → net tow measurements
```

In commercial logbooks the sample layer is usually implicit — one fishing event
produces one block of catch records — but the grammar accommodates richer
nesting without changes to the schema. The key point is that multiple sample
types share a single station record; they do not each create a new station.

### Trip-level continuous data

Some observations cannot be assigned to any specific station because they are
recorded continuously throughout the voyage: GPS/AIS position streams, VMS
pings, continuous plankton recorder (CPR) data, underway sea-surface
temperature and salinity, echograms. Historically these records were keyed by
vessel identifier and timestamp; any station link was inferred afterward from
temporal overlap.

These form a **parallel data stream** rather than a level in the hierarchy.
They are not stations (no discrete deployment or retrieval), and they are not
samples in the hierarchical sense (no parent station). In practice they should
be stored in a separate table keyed on `(vid, timestamp)` and joined to `.tid`
(and, where unambiguous, to `.sid`) after the fact. Continuous underway data
that spans multiple stations — or the entire trip — cannot be assigned to any
single station without loss of information.

In this project the AIS stream is consolidated from four sources (STK domestic
network, two commercial feeds, and AIS-corrected GPS logger positions) into
`data/trail/`, partitioned by year and accessed via `duckdbfs::open_dataset()`.
The schema is `vid, mmsi, time, lon, lat, speed, heading, cog, provider, pid`,
where `pid` is a harbour identifier assigned by spatial join — non-`NA` when the
ping falls inside a port polygon, `NA` at sea. Linking to trips uses a temporal
between-join on `(vid, T1, T2)`. See `on_ais.qmd` for the full pipeline and
data volumes.

### What belongs at station level versus sample level

The station record captures the *envelope* of an entire event: where and when
the vessel was occupied (`s1`/`s2`, `lon1`/`lat1`, `lon2`/`lat2`, `z1`/`z2`).
The sample record captures a specific observational act nested inside that
envelope — with its own instrument, method, and event-level timestamps
(`t1`–`t4`).

A practical rule: if a value characterises the station as a whole, it belongs
in the station table; if it belongs to a specific measurement act with its own
sub-structure, it belongs in a sample record.

Surface temperature illustrates the boundary case. A single temperature reading
logged automatically at the moment a station begins is a station-level attribute
(one value, no sub-structure). A full CTD profile taken at the same time is a
sample (many depth-profiled values). If the same environmental variable is
recorded by two instruments simultaneously — or if the recording instrument is
not always known — the instrument identity should be preserved as a column in
the sample record (e.g. `instrument_id`) so that conflicting readings can be
distinguished rather than silently overwritten.

Time is similarly a boundary case. The station outer bounds `s1`/`s2` define
the temporal envelope of the entire station. The event-level timestamps
`t1`–`t4` are sample-level: they belong to the specific observational act
(fishing operation, CTD cast, etc.) nested within the station. Any
finer-grained timing — sub-tow position fixes, sample-by-sample clock readings
— belongs at the measurement level. Conflating levels leads to ambiguity when
a station contains multiple concurrent samples with their own timing.



## Level 1 — Trip

A trip is the unit of a single voyage: departure from a port or base,
operations at sea, and return. For commercial vessels it corresponds to an
administrative event — a vessel licence period, a landing declaration, a crew
manifest. For research vessels it corresponds to a cruise or survey leg. The
structure is the same regardless of vessel type.

**Essential columns:**

| Column | Type | Description |
|--------|------|-------------|
| `.tid` | int / chr | Trip identifier (primary key) |
| `vid` | int / chr | Vessel identifier |
| `T1` | datetime | Departure time |
| `hid1` | int / chr | Departure harbour |
| `T2` | datetime | Landing / return time |
| `hid2` | int / chr | Landing harbour |

The `.tid` and `.sid` identifiers use a dot prefix throughout this project to
signal that they are *synthetic* keys — either assigned by the source system
or derived during processing — as opposed to natural domain keys like `vid`
(vessel number) or `sid` (species code), which have a stable external
definition.

Time columns follow a three-level naming convention that mirrors the hierarchy:

| Level | Columns | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| Trip | `T1`, `T2` | Departure and landing times (uppercase T, numbered) |
| Station | `s1`, `s2` | Outer time bounds of the station — derived as the minimum and maximum across all sample-level times at that station (lowercase s, numbered) |
| Sample | `t1`, `t2`, `t3`, `t4` | Individual event timestamps within a fishing operation or other sample (lowercase t, numbered 1–4) |

In the common commercial case where each station contains exactly one fishing
sample, `s1 = t1` and `s2 = t4` (or whichever of `t1`–`t4` are populated),
and the station outer bounds are redundant. They become necessary — and
independently meaningful — when a station contains several concurrent sample
types (e.g. a trawl haul and a CTD cast), each with its own time window.

A trip record can legitimately exist with no associated station records: a
vessel may turn back due to weather, move between harbours in ballast, or
simply have no detailed operation records in the source data. The grammar
handles this naturally — the station table is a child of the trip table, not
the other way around.

In some source systems no explicit trip table exists and must be derived.
In the Icelandic `afli` legacy schema, trips are reconstructed by grouping
stations on the same vessel sharing the same anticipated landing date and
harbour, with `.tid` assigned as the minimum `.sid` within each group.
In the EU eflalo format, the trip is likewise embedded in the logbook record
(`FT_DDAT`/`FT_DTIME`, `FT_LDAT`/`FT_LTIME`) and must be extracted and
de-duplicated: every row belonging to the same trip carries the same departure
and landing times, so `.tid` is derived via `consecutive_id()` on those columns.

## Level 2 — Station

A station is any discrete operation at a location during a trip: gear
deployment and retrieval, scientific sampling, or any other deliberate
non-transit activity. A station may contain multiple simultaneous samples of
different types (catch, hydrographic, biological); the station record holds
the envelope metadata common to all of them — principally *where* and *when*
the vessel was occupied, not *what* it was doing there.

**Essential columns:**

| Column | Type | Description |
|--------|------|-------------|
| `.sid` | int | Station identifier (primary key) |
| `.tid` | int / chr | Parent trip (foreign key) |
| `date` | date | Station date (administrative anchor; derived from `s1` when not recorded directly) |
| `s1` | datetime | Station outer start — earliest time recorded across all samples at this station |
| `s2` | datetime | Station outer end — latest time recorded across all samples at this station |
| `lon1`, `lat1` | dbl | Station spatial start (decimal degrees) |
| `lon2`, `lat2` | dbl | Station spatial end (decimal degrees) |
| `z1`, `z2` | dbl | Station depth bounds (m) |
| `sq`, `ssq` | int | Statistical square / sub-square |

`s1` and `s2` are derived outer bounds whose exact values depend on which
sample-level timestamps are populated. In the common single-fishing-sample
case `s1` maps to whichever of `t1`–`t4` is earliest (typically `t1` for
both gear classes), and `s2` to whichever is latest.

The spatial columns `lon1`/`lat1`, `lon2`/`lat2`, `z1`/`z2`, and `sq`/`ssq`
play an analogous role to `s1`/`s2`: they describe the station's spatial and
depth envelope — the bounding positions and depths across all samples at that
station, conditioned on `s1` and `s2`. This is discussed further in the
subsection below.

### The spatial envelope of a station

The position and depth columns on the station record are derived spatial
bounds in exactly the same sense as `s1`/`s2` are derived temporal bounds.
The underlying recorded positions belong to the fishing sample (or other
sample type): tow start and end coordinates, set and haul positions, depth at
gear-set and gear-haul. The station table carries these as envelope values —
the spatial extremes across all samples at the station, conditioned on
`s1`/`s2`.

The pairing convention (`lon1`/`lat1` at `s1`, `lon2`/`lat2` at `s2`) makes
the spatial columns time-ordered rather than simply min/max bounded, which
preserves the tow track direction for mobile gears. For depth, a vessel
fishing over a slope can have `z1` (depth at tow start) and `z2` (depth at
tow end) differ substantially; the pair records that range rather than
collapsing it to a single value.

`sq` and `ssq` (ICES statistical square and sub-square) are derived from
position, typically from `lon1`/`lat1` or a midpoint; they serve as coarse
spatial keys for aggregation and are station-level attributes because they
summarise the station's spatial location regardless of which sample provided
the underlying coordinates.

## Level 3 — Sample

A sample represents a discrete observational event *within* a station. A
station may contain several samples of different types recorded simultaneously
or in close succession; each is a separate row in the sample table linked by
`.sid`.

**Minimal columns for a sample table:**

| Column | Type | Description |
|--------|------|-------------|
| `.smid` | int | Sample identifier (primary key) |
| `.sid` | int | Parent station (foreign key) |
| `sample_type` | chr | Kind of observation (`"fishing"`, `"ctd"`, `"logger"`, …) |
| `t1` | datetime | Sample start — gear deployment starts |
| `t2` | datetime | Gear deployment ends (recorded for mobile gear in `afli`; absent in `fs_afladagbok` and for static gear in both schemas) |
| `t3` | datetime | Gear retrieval starts |
| `t4` | datetime | Sample end — gear retrieval ends |

### The fishing sample *(first detailed sample type)*

Of the many possible sample types — fishing operations, hydrographic casts,
biological net tows, acoustic transects, sediment grabs — the fishing sample
is the first to be fully documented here, reflecting the current project focus
on commercial fisheries logbooks. The structural principles established below
(the `t1`–`t4` event sequence, gear-specific effort, the commercial collapse
pattern) serve as a template; documentation for other sample types will follow
in later revisions of this document.

A fishing sample is a single gear deployment from which catch is recorded. Its
`t1`–`t4` timestamps and their gear-class-specific meanings are described in
the subsection below; the full column set is:


| Column | Type | Description |
|--------|------|-------------|
| `gid` | int / chr | Gear type code |
| `t1`, `t2`, `t3`, `t4` | datetime | Event timestamps (deployment start → deployment end → retrieval start → retrieval end) |
| `lon1`, `lat1` | dbl | Position at `t2` (fishing start / tow start) |
| `lon2`, `lat2` | dbl | Position at `t3` (fishing end / tow end) |
| `z1`, `z2` | dbl | Depth at fishing start and end (m) |
| `effort_count` | dbl | Count component of effort — gear-type-specific (hooks, nets, jigs, trap units; simultaneous gear count for mobile, default 1) |
| `effort_duration` | dbl | Time component of effort — in the natural unit implied by `effort_unit` (derived from capped `duration_m`) |
| `effort_unit` | chr | Unit of the product (e.g. `"gear-minutes"`, `"hook-days"`, `"net-days"`, `"jig-hours"`, `"trap-hours"`, `"setting"`) |
| `effort` | dbl | Standardised effort: `effort_count × effort_duration` |
| `towtime` | dbl | Tow duration (minutes; mobile gear) |
| `gear_width` | dbl | Effective gear width (m; mobile gear) |

Gear type (`gid`) is a fishing-sample attribute because it characterises the
specific operation performed, not the station as a location. At a station
where a trawl haul and a CTD cast are made simultaneously, the CTD sample has
no gear code; only the fishing sample does. Effort and its unit are likewise
properties of the operation: they are calculated from the fishing sample's own
timestamps (`t3 − t2`), hook counts, net counts, or trap units — none of
which are station-envelope quantities.

Gear-specific auxiliary quantities (number of hooks, nets, traps, sweeps
length, etc.) belong here too. In the source systems they appear in dedicated
sub-tables (`toga`, `lineha`, `ws_dragnot_varpa`, `ws_linanethandf`, etc.)
that are normalised for the relational database. In the unified schema they
are flattened as nullable columns on the fishing sample record: populated for
the relevant gear class, `NA` elsewhere.

**Commercial logbook collapse.** In commercial logbook data there is typically
exactly one fishing sample per station, so the sample level is not represented
as a separate table in practice. All fishing-sample columns — `gid`, `t1`–`t4`,
position, depth, `effort_count`, `effort_duration`, `effort_unit`, `effort`, `towtime`, `gear_width` — appear
directly on the station record as denormalised columns. The grammar locates
them at the sample level regardless of where they are physically stored;
downstream code should treat them as sample attributes even when they live in
the station table.

### The time structure of a fishing sample

Any fishing operation — regardless of gear class — passes through four
discrete physical events. The grammar labels these `t1` through `t4` in the
order they occur. No single logbook system records all four for all gear
types, but naming them consistently is essential for cross-gear comparisons,
effort calculations, and joining vessel-tracking data to fishing activity.

#### Event 1 — Gear deployment starts (`t1`)

- *Static gear* (longline, gillnet, trap): First buoy enters the water.
- *Mobile gear* (trawl, seine, dredge): Gear enters the water.

#### Event 2 — Gear deployment ends (`t2`)

- *Static gear*: Last buoy enters the water — the full gear is set and the
  soak period begins.
- *Mobile gear*: Gear reaches the bottom (or fishing depth for pelagic
  trawls). This is the start of active fishing, the start of the effort
  interval, and the point from which vessel-tracking positions are assigned
  to the fishing operation.

#### Event 3 — Gear retrieval starts (`t3`)

- *Static gear*: First buoy leaves the water — hauling begins. This is the
  point from which vessel-tracking positions are assigned to the fishing
  operation.
- *Mobile gear*: Gear leaves the bottom (or fishing depth). This is the end
  of active fishing and the end of the effort interval; vessel-tracking
  positions are no longer assigned to the fishing operation after this point.

#### Event 4 — Gear retrieval ends (`t4`)

- *Static gear*: Last buoy leaves the water. Vessel-tracking positions are no
  longer assigned to the fishing operation after this point.
- *Mobile gear*: Gear is fully out of the water.

::: {.callout-note}
**Soak time and hook-level variation (static gear).** There is no single
"start of active fishing" at the gear level — each hook or mesh segment
enters and leaves the water at a different time during the deployment and
retrieval phases. Because retrieval typically takes longer than deployment,
the first hook deployed also has the shortest individual soak time, and the
last hook the longest. In principle a mean soak time per hook could be
reconstructed from the four event times, but in practice it is the four
gear-level timestamps that are recorded.
:::

#### The AIS location window

Joining vessel-tracking positions to a fishing operation requires knowing
during which interval the vessel's position is informative about *where
fishing occurred*. This interval differs between gear classes:

- **Mobile gear:** The vessel tows the gear throughout `[t2, t3]`, so vessel
  position equals gear position during the entire effort interval. The
  location window is `[t2, t3]`.
- **Static gear:** The gear fishes autonomously during the soak; the vessel
  may be anywhere. The vessel is physically co-located with the gear only
  during retrieval. The location window is therefore `[t3, t4]`.

This asymmetry — the window shifts by one interval between gear classes —
creates awkwardness if AIS joins are written separately per gear class. The
practical fix is to precompute two derived columns, `w1` and `w2`, once in
`fishing_sample`:

| | Mobile | Static |
|---|---|---|
| `w1` (location window start) | `t2` | `t3` |
| `w2` (location window end) | `t3` | `t4` |

All downstream joins then operate uniformly on `[w1, w2]`, with the
gear-class logic encapsulated in one place. In this project the vessel
positions are sourced from `data/trail/` (see `on_ais.qmd`), joined as:

```r
fishing_sample |>
  left_join(
    open_dataset("data/trail/"),
    by = join_by(vid, between(time, w1, w2))
  )
```

#### Effort duration

The theoretically correct effort interval is `t3 − t2` for all gear classes:
deployment-end to retrieval-start. For mobile gear in the `afli` schema, t2 is
derived from `ibotni` (the recorded gear-on-bottom time) and t3 from t2 +
logged duration, so the interval is exact. In `fs_afladagbok`, t3 is absent
for mobile gear (OTB/OTM/DRB); duration is approximated as `t4 − t1` (full
operation from warp-entry to retrieval end), which overestimates the active
tow time by the warp-out and warp-in intervals. For static gear, `t2` (last
hook/net in) is never recorded in either schema; soak time is therefore
approximated as `t3 − t1` — the interval from first-hook-in to first-hook-hauled
— which overestimates true soak time by the deployment duration `t2 − t1`.

#### Non-overlap constraints

Deployment windows `[t1, t2]` cannot overlap across concurrent operations on
the same vessel; a vessel can only be in one place setting gear at a time.
Retrieval windows `[t3, t4]` cannot overlap either. The *outer* interval
`[t1, t4]` of two operations *can* overlap legitimately for static gear —
deploying a new longline while an older one soaks is normal practice:

```
Operation A:  t1_A ── t2_A · · · · · · · · · t3_A ── t4_A
Operation B:               t1_B ── t2_B · · · · · · · · · t3_B ── t4_B
```

The correct data-quality checks are therefore:

- `t1 < t2 < t3 < t4` must hold within every individual operation (with `t2`
  between `t1` and `t3` when recorded).
- No two operations on the same vessel share an overlapping `[t3, t4]`
  retrieval window.
- No two operations on the same vessel share an overlapping `[t1, t2]`
  deployment window — but since `t2` is rarely recorded for static gear,
  this check is limited in practice to detecting identical `t1` timestamps.

A check that flags any two operations with overlapping `[t1, t4]` outer
windows will produce false positives for static gear and should not be used.

#### What is typically recorded

The table below summarises what is available in practice across the two source
schemas. Gear code entry errors exist in both systems — a record nominally coded
as mobile gear may occasionally represent a static operation and vice versa —
so the timestamp pattern of a record is a useful cross-check on the declared
gear code.

| Label | Mobile (trawl/dredge) | Jig / handline | Static (net / longline) |
|---|---|---|---|
| `t1` | `afli`: absent; `fs_afladagbok`: always present | Rarely | Usually (`afli`); always present (`fs_afladagbok`) |
| `t2` | `afli`: derived from `ibotni` (gear-on-bottom hhmm); `fs_afladagbok`: absent | Rarely | Never in either schema |
| `t3` | `afli`: derived (t2 + duration); `fs_afladagbok`: absent for OTB/OTM/DRB, always = t1 for SDN | Rarely | Usually (`afli`); always present (`fs_afladagbok`) |
| `t4` | `afli`: absent; `fs_afladagbok`: always present | Rarely | Usually (`afli`); always present (`fs_afladagbok`) |

For jigs, the canonical expectation is t2 and t3 (fishing start and end); with
three distinct timestamps the interpretation is t1, t2, t3. In practice the
`afli` jig records carry timestamps in only ~3% of rows, so effort is almost
always derived from the logged duration (`klst`) rather than timestamp
differences.

### Other sample types

A sample-level table becomes necessary the moment more than one type of
observation is made at the same station:

- A scientific survey tow may record both a catch sample (species composition
  and weight) and a series of hydrographic casts (CTD profiles) at the same
  station position. These are two distinct samples with their own `t1`/`t4`
  bounds.
- Continuous electronic logger data — position, speed, heading, net opening,
  depth — recorded during a tow constitute a third type of sample: a time
  series of sensor readings rather than a single aggregate measurement.

The `afli` legacy system implicitly recognises this: `stofn.parquet` is the
station table, while `rafr_sjalfvirkir_maelar.parquet` is a separate table of
automatic logger readings linked to the same station via `visir` (`.sid`). The
logger table is a sample in the grammar's sense — a different observation type,
at a finer temporal resolution, recorded alongside the main fishing operation.

### The sensor layer — multiple tables at different levels

The logbook project currently maintains two sensor-level tables for the `afli`
schema, and their structural difference is instructive:

| Table | Rows | Has `time`? | Grammar level |
|---|--:|:--:|---|
| `data-raw/logbooks/afli/sensor.parquet` | ~3.9 M | No | Station supplement |
| `data-raw/logbooks/afli/sensor_GPS.parquet` | ~27.7 M | Yes | Measurement (time-series) |

`sensor.parquet` carries one row per station — gear flags, surface/bottom/
headline temperatures, headline depth — with no timestamp column. It is a
**station-level supplement**: sensor readings that have already been aggregated
or summarised to the station envelope. It belongs conceptually alongside the
station record, not inside a time-series.

`sensor_GPS.parquet` carries many rows per station, each with a timestamp and
a position (`lon`/`lat`, corrected from AIS), plus speed, heading, depth, and
temperature channels. It is a **measurement-level time-series**: one row per
logger tick, keyed on `(.tid, .sid, vid, time)`. The original wacky coordinates
are retained as `w_lon`/`w_lat`; `dt_sec` records the interpolation interval
used in the AIS-based position correction. The AIS positions used for the
correction are drawn from `data/trail/` — the consolidated multi-source track
dataset described in `on_ais.qmd`.

This table has a dual role: it is simultaneously a measurement-level logbook
supplement (linked to stations via `.sid`) and one of the four sources merged
into `data/trail/` under `provider = "wacky"`. In the consolidated AIS trail
the records appear with corrected coordinates and no station link; in
`sensor_GPS.parquet` the same records carry `.sid` and `.tid` keys and the
original wacky coordinates alongside the corrected ones.

This split reflects a real structural difference that the grammar should make
explicit rather than paper over with a single "sensor" label. A single physical
logger records multiple channels — GPS, depth, temperature, heading — at a
common tick rate. But other instruments (e.g. NMEA-connected sensors: net
mensuration systems, echo sounders, flow-through temperature chains) may operate
at different sampling rates and produce separate data streams. Each stream is
a candidate for its own measurement-level table, keyed on `.sid` and a
timestamp, with `instrument_id` identifying the physical source.

**Consequence for downstream code.** The two tables are not interchangeable.
Use `sensor_GPS.parquet` for position time-series and spatial analysis; use
`sensor.parquet` for station-level environmental covariates. Where
`sensor_GPS.parquet` has `NA` lon/lat (AIS coverage absent), fall back to
station-level position from `station.parquet`.

## Level 4 — Observation / Measurement

At the lowest level sit the individual observations or measurements produced
by a sample. What these look like depends entirely on the sample type:

- For a **fishing sample**: one row per species per sample (or per station when
  the sample level is collapsed), recording species identity and catch weight.
- For a **hydrographic sample**: one row per depth bin, recording temperature,
  salinity, oxygen, etc.
- For a **biological sample**: one row per individual organism, recording
  length, weight, age, sex.
- For a **logger sample**: one row per timestamp, recording position, speed,
  depth, net opening, etc.

The grammar does not prescribe the internal structure of measurement tables
beyond requiring a foreign key to the parent sample (`.smid`) or, when the
sample level is collapsed, to the parent station (`.sid`).

**Catch table (commercial fishing):**

| Column | Type | Description |
|--------|------|-------------|
| `.sid` | int | Parent station (foreign key; or `.smid` if sample table is present) |
| `sid` | int / chr | Species identifier |
| `catch` | dbl | Landed weight (kg) |

Note the deliberate naming distinction: `.sid` (dot-prefixed) is the station
synthetic key; `sid` (no dot) is the species code — a stable external
identifier from the species vocabulary. The two happen to share the stem "sid"
for different reasons, and the dot prefix is the only disambiguation needed.

## Landings — a parallel data stream *(commercial fisheries)*

The following section is specific to commercial fisheries; it has no direct
equivalent for scientific survey voyages. It also serves as the prototype for
how a *parallel project* plugs into the grammar: a distinct institutional data
source with its own schema, linked to the logbook hierarchy by shared natural
keys rather than by shared processing code.

Landings records are not a level within the observation hierarchy; they are a
parallel stream collected independently of the logbook by a third party — a
harbour authority, fish buyer, or national statistics body. Their natural keys
are the same that anchor a trip: vessel (`vid`), landing date/time (`T2`), and
landing harbour (`hid2`). A synthetic `.lid` can be assigned after
de-duplication, but the link to a specific trip is made via those natural keys,
not via `.tid`.

```
Trip  ──(vid, T2, hid2)──→  Landing (.lid)
 └── Station
      └── Catch       ←──── should sum to ────→  Landing catch (by species)
```

The catch table is the skipper's self-reported account of what was caught at
each station; the landing record is the buyer's or authority's account of what
arrived at the dock. In an ideal system `sum(catch)` across all stations of a
trip equals the landed quantity for each species. Discrepancies arise from
at-sea discards, mis-reporting, species misidentification, or processing
losses. Tracking the difference is a data quality diagnostic: the grammar keeps
the two streams structurally separate precisely so the discrepancy is
measurable.

**Minimal columns for a landings table:**

| Column | Type | Description |
|--------|------|-------------|
| `.lid` | int / chr | Landing identifier (primary key) |
| `vid` | int / chr | Vessel identifier |
| `T2` | datetime | Landing date/time |
| `hid2` | int / chr | Landing harbour |
| `sid` | int / chr | Species identifier |
| `catch` | dbl | Landed weight (kg) |

Landings records sometimes carry limited spatial information — a statistical
rectangle or fishing area declared on the landing form — but this is far
coarser than the GPS positions on a logbook station. When linkage to a trip
succeeds, the richer station-level spatial information is preferred; the
landing-form area serves only as a fallback.

## The column vocabulary

The grammar is only as useful as its naming conventions are consistent. The
table below lists the core column names; collectively they form a controlled
vocabulary that any downstream script can rely on without inspecting the source
schema.

| Name | Level | Meaning |
|------|-------|---------|
| `.tid` | Trip | Synthetic trip key |
| `.sid` | Station | Synthetic station key |
| `vid` | Trip | Vessel identifier |
| `cid` | Trip | Country identifier |
| `T1`, `T2` | Trip | Departure / landing time (uppercase = trip level) |
| `hid1`, `hid2` | Trip | Departure / landing harbour |
| `n_crew` | Trip | Crew count |
| `date` | Station | Station date (administrative anchor) |
| `s1`, `s2` | Station | Outer time bounds (derived — min/max of sample times) |
| `lon1`, `lat1` | Station | Spatial start (envelope — position at `s1`) |
| `lon2`, `lat2` | Station | Spatial end (envelope — position at `s2`) |
| `z1`, `z2` | Station | Depth bounds (envelope — depth at `s1`/`s2`) |
| `sq`, `ssq` | Station | Statistical square / sub-square (derived from position) |
| `gid` | (Fishing) Sample | Gear type code |
| `t1`, `t2`, `t3`, `t4` | (Fishing) Sample | Deployment start / deployment end / retrieval start / retrieval end |
| `w1`, `w2` | (Fishing) Sample | AIS location window: `[t2, t3]` for mobile gear; `[t3, t4]` for static gear — derived, used for vessel-track joins |
| `effort_count` | (Fishing) Sample | Count component of effort (hooks, nets, jigs, trap units, or simultaneous gear count for mobile) |
| `effort_duration` | (Fishing) Sample | Time component of effort (in the natural unit for each gear type; derived from capped `duration_m`) |
| `effort_unit` | (Fishing) Sample | Unit of the product (e.g. `"gear-minutes"`, `"hook-days"`, `"net-days"`, `"setting"`) |
| `effort` | (Fishing) Sample | Standardised effort: `effort_count × effort_duration` |
| `towtime` | (Fishing) Sample | Tow duration (minutes) |
| `gear_width` | (Fishing) Sample | Effective gear width (m) |
| `sid` | Catch | Species identifier |
| `catch` | Catch | Catch weight (kg) |

Three naming conventions are worth making explicit:

- **Dot-prefix** on `.tid`, `.sid`, `.smid` marks synthetic or derived keys,
  not natural domain identifiers. Columns generated inside processing functions
  — and not present in the raw input — also carry a dot prefix (e.g. `.intv`,
  `.checks`).
- **Three-level time convention** makes the temporal hierarchy unambiguous at
  a glance: uppercase `T1`/`T2` are trip bounds; lowercase `s1`/`s2` are
  station outer bounds (derived from sample times); lowercase `t1`–`t4` are
  sample-level event timestamps (defined in detail for fishing samples; the
  same sequence applies to other sample types as appropriate).
- **Numeric suffix** on paired columns (`lon1`/`lon2`, `lat1`/`lat2`,
  `z1`/`z2`, `hid1`/`hid2`) consistently encodes start (1) and end (2),
  regardless of which physical direction "start" implies for a given gear type.

## Resolution and the hierarchy

Not all data systems resolve the hierarchy to the same depth. The grammar
describes the ideal; real-world data falls somewhere along a resolution
continuum from individual fishing operation to broad annual totals. The table
below places the main data types encountered in practice on that continuum:

| Resolution level | Typical grouping keys | Effort recorded | Spatial precision | Example source |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Station** | `.sid`, `s1`/`s2`, GPS position | Per operation | Metres | Icelandic electronic logbooks |
| **Event** | trip × gear × day × rectangle | Implied by trip duration | ICES rectangle (~50 km) | EU eflalo |
| **Monthly aggregate** | year, month, vessel, gear, harbour, rectangle, species | Hours / sets per month | ICES rectangle | Pre-electronic Icelandic records |
| **Annual aggregate** | year, species, area | None or coarse | Large statistical area | Pre-logbook statistics |

### Station level

The station-level case is discussed throughout this document. Each row
represents a single, discrete operation — a gear deployment, an instrument
cast, an acoustic transect leg. Position and timing are recorded at operation
resolution; effort is recorded for fishing operations specifically.

### Event level (EU eflalo)

What appears in an eflalo row as a single observation — a gear deployed on a
given date in a given rectangle — may in reality represent several distinct
fishing operations. A trawler working the same grounds all day in the same ICES
rectangle produces one eflalo row regardless of whether it made two hauls or
ten. The station level is simply not recorded; only the aggregate is.

This is not a deficiency unique to EU reporting: many national logbook systems
operate at the same resolution, and even some Icelandic vessels in the early
`afli` period reported only date and rectangle without timestamps or GPS. The
eflalo format merely formalises this aggregate reporting as a standard.

The wide catch format used in eflalo is a separate structural choice with
practical consequences: pivoting from `LE_KG_<SP>` columns to long format (one
row per species) is a required preprocessing step before any species-level
analysis, and the number of species columns varies across submissions and years,
making automated processing fragile.

### Monthly and annual aggregates

Historical data is often available only in aggregated form — the original
station-level records were never digitised, or have been lost. A typical
pre-electronic Icelandic record might contain: year, month, vessel, gear type,
landing harbour, ICES rectangle, species, total catch, and total effort
(expressed as hours towed, or number of sets). This is structurally richer than
eflalo — effort and catch are both present — but finer temporal or spatial
resolution is unrecoverable.

At even coarser resolution, effort disappears entirely: records contain only
year, species, and a large geographic area (e.g. Iceland total, or North
Atlantic FAO area). These are the earliest layers of the historical record.

### Joint analysis across resolution levels

A researcher studying long-run trends in catch rates or gear efficiency needs
to work with station-level data for recent decades alongside monthly aggregates
for historical years. The four-level hierarchy is defined at station resolution;
it cannot directly accommodate monthly aggregates without blurring the
distinction between a row that represents one tow and a row that represents
a month's worth of tows.

Two approaches are available:

**Option A — Separate tables, harmonised by a view.** Keep the station table at
its native resolution and store historical aggregates in a separate
`aggregate.parquet` table keyed on (year, month, vessel, gear, harbour,
rectangle, species). For joint analysis, a query projects both onto a common
schema — typically the monthly aggregate resolution — by grouping station
records before stacking:

```r
# Approximate sketch — not production code
station |>
  mutate(year = year(date), month = month(date)) |>
  summarise(
    catch = sum(catch), effort = sum(effort),
    .by = c(vid, year, month, gid, hid2, sq, sid)
  ) |>
  bind_rows(aggregate)
```

This keeps the raw tables unambiguous and lets the analyst choose the
aggregation level. It is the preferred approach when the station table is the
primary data source.

**Option B — Synthetic stations and trips for aggregate records.** Each row in
an aggregate table can be treated as a pseudo-station: a synthetic `.sid` is
assigned; `lon1`/`lat1` are set to the ICES rectangle centroid; `t2` is the
first day of the month and `t3` the last (or both are left `NA`); effort is
taken directly from the recorded total. A synthetic `.tid` is likewise
assigned — one per (vessel, month) combination when vessel is known, one per
aggregate row otherwise. This preserves the single-table grammar and allows
downstream scripts to operate on any source without special-casing, at the
cost of introducing fictitious precision that must be clearly flagged.

The two options are not mutually exclusive. A practical workflow might store
historical aggregates in their native form (Option A) and materialise synthetic
stations only when feeding a downstream analysis tool that expects a station
table.

### A concrete case: monthly aggregates embedded in `afli/stofn`

The `afli` schema illustrates a common real-world complication: aggregate
records were stored in the *same table* as individual-operation records, with
no structural separation — only a free-text `comment` field distinguishing them.
Three batches are present:

| `comment` value | Species group | `gid` | Records | Date range |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| `Frá OPS$SIGFUS.GAMALL_HUMAR, samantekt` | Nephrops | 9 | 26,405 | 1960–1999 |
| `Frá OPS$SIGFUS.GOMUL_RAEKJA, samantekt` | Shrimp | 14 | 12,708 | 1953–1986 |
| `Frá afli.gomul_dragnot, samantekt, ...` | D-seine | 5 | 1,854 | 1960–1966 |

All three have `date` fixed to the 15th of the month (a clear mid-month
placeholder), no coordinates, and exactly one catch row per record — all
consistent with monthly summaries rather than individual hauls. They were
identified by inspecting `count(comment)` on the raw table before any
downstream processing.

These records are excluded from `station.parquet` and `fishing_sample.parquet`
and stored separately as `data/afli/aggregate.parquet` (Option A), a flat table
keyed on `.sid × species` that also carries aggregated tow time from the `toga`
detail table. The original `.sid` is preserved so any record can be traced back
to the Oracle source.

The practical lesson: always inspect free-text annotation columns early in
a convert script. Aggregate rows mixed into a station table will silently
inflate record counts, distort effort distributions, and corrupt swept-area
calculations if not removed.

### `station_type` — making provenance explicit

Regardless of which option is chosen, the station table should carry a
`station_type` column that records the provenance and resolution of each row:

| Value | Meaning |
|---|---|
| `"operation"` | Genuine individual station-level operation (logbook station, survey cast, etc.) |
| `"event"` | Aggregate: trip × gear × day × rectangle (EU eflalo style) |
| `"monthly_aggregate"` | Aggregate over one calendar month |
| `"annual_aggregate"` | Aggregate over one calendar year |
| `"reconstructed"` | Station derived from VMS / AIS ping classification |

Any analysis sensitive to the distinction — swept-area calculations, temporal
overlap checks, tow-time distributions — should filter or weight by
`station_type`. Analyses that are resolution-agnostic (e.g. total catch by
species and year) can ignore it.

The `station_type` column also serves the purpose proposed in "Scope and
extensions": flagging reconstructed stations derived from VMS / AIS pings
alongside the other provenance classes.

## Reconstructing station resolution from position data

When logbook data only records events (trip × gear × day × rectangle), station-
level spatial and temporal resolution can be partially recovered by linking
logbook records to high-frequency vessel position data — either VMS (Vessel
Monitoring System) pings, transmitted at 2-hour intervals, or AIS
(Automatic Identification System) pings, available at much higher frequency
(minutes or seconds for many vessels).

The reconstruction proceeds in two steps:

**Step 1 — Activity classification.** Not every ping represents active fishing;
vessels also steam between grounds, enter harbour, drift at anchor, and shelter
from weather. Fishing activity is inferred from vessel speed: a trawler moving
at 2–4 knots is likely towing gear; the same vessel at 10 knots is steaming.
Speed thresholds by gear type are estimated from the bimodal speed distribution
of fishing versus steaming, typically fit using a Gaussian mixture model
[@woillez2016]. Pings classified as "fishing" become implicit station records
at the temporal and spatial resolution of the position data.

**Step 2 — Catch distribution.** Logbook catch, recorded at the event level
(trip × gear × day × rectangle), must be distributed across the individual
fishing pings that fall within that event. The simplest approach assigns equal
catch weight to each ping; more sophisticated methods weight by swept area,
gear width, or tow duration.

This two-step procedure is the foundation of the ICES VMS and logbook data-call
workflow, implemented in the `osfd` R package [@osfd]. The key structural point
is that the procedure is a *consequence* of the station level being absent from
the logbook data: were individual fishing operations recorded with timestamps
and GPS positions — as they are in the Icelandic high-resolution system — no
catch distribution step would be needed. The catch would already be attributed
to a precise time and place.

The high-resolution Icelandic data illustrates what the EU data is trying to
approximate. A bottom trawler with full electronic logbook coverage has `t2`,
`t3`, `lon1`/`lat1`, `lon2`/`lat2` for every haul: the station table is
complete. The same vessel operating under EU reporting requirements would
produce a single eflalo row for the day, and a reconstructed station table
derived from VMS pings would be the best available substitute. The grammar
is the same; the provenance of the station records differs.

VMS and AIS pings themselves form a natural sample-level table in the grammar:
they are repeated positional observations associated with a station (fishing
event), at finer temporal resolution than the logbook entry. In the `osfd`
workflow, processed pings carry `.pid` (ping identifier), `vid`, `time`, `lon`,
`lat`, `speed`, and `heading` — a table that maps directly onto the sample
level of the hierarchy, with the classified fishing pings serving as implicit
station boundaries. The `data/trail/` dataset described in `on_ais.qmd`
provides exactly this structure for Icelandic vessels (`vid, mmsi, time, lon,
lat, speed, heading, cog, provider, pid`), consolidating ~566 million pings
across 2007–2026 from four sources into a single Hive-partitioned parquet
store.

## How data systems map onto the grammar

| | `afli` | `fs_afladagbok` | `adb`^†^ | EU eflalo |
|-|--------|-----------------|-------|-----------|
| **Trip key** | Derived: `min(.sid)` per `(vid, T2, hid2)` | `ws_veidiferd.id` | `trip_v.trip_id` | Derived: `consecutive_id()` on `FT_DDAT`/`FT_LDAT` |
| **Station key** | `stofn.visir` | `ws_veidi.id` | `station_v.station_id` | `.eid` (row number — not a true station) |
| **Station concept** | Individual fishing operation | Individual fishing operation | Individual fishing operation | Aggregate: trip × gear × day × rectangle |
| **Gear code** | Legacy integer (old) | New integer (canonical) | New integer (derived) | ICES metier string (e.g. `OTB`, `LLS`) |
| **Spatial resolution** | GPS (metres) | GPS (metres) | GPS (variable quality) | ICES rectangle (~50 km) |
| **Temporal resolution** | Minutes (on-bottom clock) | Minutes (electronic timestamps) | Minutes (electronic timestamps) | Day (`LE_CDAT`); rarely `t2`/`t3` |
| **Catch format** | Long | Long | Long | Wide (`LE_KG_<SP>` columns) |

^†^ `adb` is the live-streaming feed and is **excluded from the merged output** (`data/logbooks/`): it uses old gear codes and has less reliable coordinates. It is documented here for completeness.

The gear code unification for the Icelandic schemas is handled by
`gear_mapping` (`data/gear/gear_mapping.parquet`), which maps the legacy many-
to-one from old codes to new. For the EU eflalo system, gear is expressed as
an ICES metier string (`LE_GEAR`, `LE_MET`) that encodes gear type, target
species, and mesh size, and maps onto the same ICES controlled vocabulary via
`sfdSAR::predict_gear_width()` for swept-area calculations.

### Raw column mapping by grammar level

The table below is generated from `data/dictionary.parquet` and shows, for
every unified column name, which raw source column it originates from in each
of the three Icelandic schemas. Grammar level annotations (`trip`, `station`,
`sample`, `catch`) are currently assigned for the `afli` schema; the same
levels apply to the corresponding rows in the other two schemas wherever the
unified name matches.

For `afli`, the source table is shown in parentheses because several unified
names are populated from more than one raw table (e.g. `temp1_surface` appears
in both `toga` and `lineha`). A dash (—) means the variable is absent or not
yet mapped in that schema.

```{r}
#| label: tbl-column-mapping
#| tbl-cap: "Raw-to-unified column mapping by grammar level across the three Icelandic logbook schemas. `afli` entries show `raw_name (source_table)`."
library(dplyr)
library(nanoparquet)
library(knitr)

dict <- read_parquet("data/dictionary.parquet")

# Grammar level comes from afli annotations
afli_levels <- dict |>
  filter(schema == "afli", !is.na(level)) |>
  select(clean, level) |>
  distinct()

# Build per-schema display strings; include source table for afli
raw_names <- dict |>
  filter(schema %in% c("afli", "fs_afladagbok", "adb")) |>
  mutate(display = if_else(
    schema == "afli",
    paste0(messy, " (", table, ")"),
    messy
  )) |>
  summarise(display = paste(unique(display), collapse = ", "),
            .by = c(clean, schema)) |>
  tidyr::pivot_wider(names_from = schema, values_from = display,
                     values_fill = "—")

afli_levels |>
  left_join(raw_names, by = "clean") |>
  arrange(factor(level, levels = c("trip", "station", "sample", "catch")),
          clean) |>
  select(level, clean, afli, fs_afladagbok, adb) |>
  rename(Level = level,
         `Unified` = clean,
         `afli raw (table)` = afli,
         `fs_afladagbok raw` = fs_afladagbok,
         `adb raw` = adb) |>
  kable()
```

## Scope and extensions

The grammar described here is intentionally minimal. The four levels and the
column vocabulary above are sufficient to represent commercial logbook data
across all gear types and reporting resolutions encountered in practice — from
the full-detail Icelandic system to the aggregate EU eflalo format. The grammar
also accommodates scientific survey data with minor additions at the sample and
measurement levels (sample type, individual fish measurements, hydrographic
observations).

What the grammar does *not* prescribe is the encoding of controlled
vocabularies: which codes are valid gear types, which harbour identifiers are
valid, which species codes are in use. Those are lookup tables —
`gear_mapping`, the harbour register, the species list, the ICES metier
vocabulary — that sit alongside the grammar tables but are not part of the
core hierarchy. Keeping them separate means the grammar itself is stable even
as vocabularies evolve.

Three extensions are of immediate practical interest:

1. **Formalise the sensor layer.** The two existing sensor tables
   (`sensor.parquet` as station supplement, `sensor_GPS.parquet` as position
   time-series) already expose the multi-table, multi-resolution nature of the
   sensor layer. As NMEA-sourced instrument streams are formalised — dedicated
   tables per sensor type at their native sampling rate — a formal sample
   identifier (`.smid`) will be needed to link concurrent sensor sessions to the
   same station cleanly and to make the four-level hierarchy fully consistent
   across all data types.

2. **`station_type` provenance flag.** A `station_type` column on the station
   table records whether a row represents a genuine individual fishing operation,
   an aggregate logbook event, a historically aggregated record, or a station
   reconstructed from VMS / AIS pings. The full vocabulary and its role in
   joint analysis across resolution levels is described in the
   [Resolution and the hierarchy](#resolution-and-the-hierarchy) section.

3. **Parallel projects within the same grammar.** The logbook project is one of
   several anticipated data projects that share this four-level structure and
   column vocabulary:

   - **Landings.** Landing declarations — vessel, date, harbour, species, weight
     — are the natural counterpart to logbook catch (see
     [Landings](#landings-a-parallel-data-stream)). A dedicated landings project
     would formalise the institutional data sources, de-duplication logic, and
     linkage to logbook trips that mirror what the logbook project does for
     at-sea records.
   - **Oceanographic and biological surveys.** Research vessels collecting
     hydrographic, acoustic, and biological data operate within the same
     hierarchy. The grammar was designed from the outset to accommodate these
     alongside fishing data; the sample-type vocabulary (`"ctd"`, `"trawl"`,
     `"acoustic"`, etc.) provides the hook.
   - **Continuous underway data.** Sea-surface temperature, salinity, and
     acoustics recorded throughout a voyage fit naturally in the measurement-
     level time-series pattern already illustrated by `sensor_GPS.parquet`.

   All parallel projects share the same column vocabulary and key conventions.
   Cross-project joins — logbook catch linked to landing declarations by
   `(vid, T2, hid2)`, research survey stations overlaid on commercial fishing
   grounds, continuous underway data attributed to logbook trips — require no
   schema translation.
